summary:
The Deccan Sultanates (c. 1490–1686 AD) were five successor states to the Bahmani Sultanate that dominated the central Deccan plateau of India before their eventual absorption by the [[event:103|Mughal Empire]]: Bijapur, Ahmednagar, Golconda, Bidar, and Berar. These sultanates, though Muslim-ruled, developed distinctive syncretic cultures blending Persian, Arabic, Turkish, and Hindu elements, produced significant architectural achievements (the Gol Gumbaz mausoleum in Bijapur, with the world's second-largest pre-modern dome, and the Charminar in Hyderabad), and patronized Dakhni Urdu — the earliest form of the Urdu language. They were frequently in conflict both with each other and with the Hindu [[event:2509|Vijayanagara Empire]] to their south.

The Deccan Sultanates cooperated briefly and successfully in 1565, allying to defeat and destroy the Vijayanagara Empire at the [[event:2509|Battle of Talikota]] — eliminating the last major Hindu empire of medieval India. Thereafter, Mughal pressure from the north and inter-sultanate rivalry gradually weakened them. Emperor [[event:2507|Aurangzeb]] spent the last 27 years of his reign (1681–1707) in the Deccan, systematically conquering Bijapur (1686) and Golconda (1687) after decades of warfare — a campaign that achieved its territorial objective but exhausted Mughal resources, contributed to the empire's financial overextension, and ultimately weakened the Mughal state by drawing it into endless guerrilla conflict with the [[event:2368|Maratha Empire]], accelerating the [[event:2446|Mughal decline]].
